After using the Samsung Galaxy S25 FE for over a week, I can confidently say it’s one of the best value for money Samsung phones right now.

For just $500, you’re getting:
• a smooth and polished UI
• solid, premium build quality
• impressive main and ultrawide cameras
• loud and clear speakers
• a great display

The downside of the Samsung Galaxy S25 FE is its pretty average battery life. If you’re a heavy user, you’ll likely need to charge it twice a day, and also the Exynos 2400 heats up quite easily on outdoors.

tweets.text:
iPhone 13 vs iPhone 15 Camera Test — Is the Upgrade REALLY Worth It? 📸

At first glance, they look similar. But once you press the shutter, the difference becomes clear.

The iPhone 13 uses a 12MP main camera, while the iPhone 15 jumps to 48MP, delivering up to 4x more detail. That means sharper images, cleaner crops, and significantly better zoom results without losing quality.

And speaking of zoom, the iPhone 15 introduces 2x lossless zoom powered by its high-resolution sensor. No telephoto lens, yet it still achieves optical-quality results. On the iPhone 13, zooming in quickly reduces detail.

Low-light photography is another major shift. While both devices support Night Mode, the iPhone 15 is backed by a more advanced image processing system. The result is better light balance, more natural skin tones, and improved shadow detail.

Color and dynamic range also see noticeable improvements. The iPhone 13 delivers solid images, but the iPhone 15 produces richer colors, stronger contrast, and more accurate highlights, closer to what your eyes actually see.

Portrait photography has evolved too. The iPhone 15 offers improved edge detection, more realistic background blur, and the ability to adjust focus after taking the shot, something the iPhone 13 can’t match in complex scenes.

For video, both shoot in 4K and perform well, but the iPhone 15 benefits from better stabilization, cleaner HDR, and more consistent results in challenging lighting conditions.

So what’s the verdict?

If you take casual photos, the iPhone 13 still holds up. But if you care about detail, zoom, low-light performance, and content-quality output, the iPhone 15 is a clear upgrade.

This isn’t just a camera improvement. It’s the difference between taking photos and creating content.

Which one are you choosing, iPhone 13 or iPhone 15? 👇

#iPhone15 #iPhone13 #Apple #TechReview #SalsaTech

Facing battery issues on my Acer Predator Helios 300 for the past 2 years. Unable to get an original battery as no service center has it. Disappointed with Acer India’s after-sales support. Need urgent help with genuine battery replacement. @Acer_India  @VenomsTech 
@Acer Part 1

I've been quietly working on Distropy, an open-source LLM inference server written in Rust.

While running some final optimization tests with VS Code + GitHub Chat (which loves sending huge context even on empty chats), I got this result and had to share:

Model: Qwen3-0.6B-Q4_K_M
GPU: RTX 4070 12GB
Query: "what is vue"

First request:
Prefill: 12,007 tokens in 742 ms → 16,181 tokens/sec

Second request (same conversation):
Prefill: only 243 tokens
prefix_cached: 12,003 tokens
Prefill time: 4 ms → 60,750 tokens/sec

Total end-to-end latency: 175 ms

I went from 10–20 seconds of painful prefill on every request down to under 200ms total. The difference is night and day.

The key was getting KV prefix caching working properly with llama.cpp. Once the large static prefix (system prompt + tools) is cached, subsequent requests become extremely cheap.

I'm getting close to an initial release, and seeing this kind of performance gives me a lot of confidence.
Would love to hear your thoughts — especially if you've also struggled with massive repeated tool schemas and context from IDEs.

Let me know if you'd be interested in trying it when it's ready.

profile_bio.description: MIT UX | Eng | Building @distropyAI | AI founder pivoting hard | Now cooking something bigger

tweets.text:
The most useful feature I see on the @GeminiApp is to paste a youtube link and ask for a summary, saves a lot of time and it's easier to parse.
It's also pretty fast, you can tell their TPUs go brrrr...
I also set up the Gemini system prompt with a private link to a Google Sheets with my budget, so I can ask questions about it.
I also use it to create tasks (Google Tasks/Google Keep).
I rarely use the feature of taking a screenshot on my Pixel phone to ask questions about it.
In summary: is it worth it to use a Pixel 9 Pro XL? Sure, for the camera, the extra battery, and extra screen size to play Balatro and Polytopia (lol).
But is it worth it for the AI features? Nah.
In general, I'm still using ChatGPT/Codex, pretty good price/quality, and only Gemini for stuff that's integrated with the Google ecosystem.
I still think that making you run out of tokens is a great business model, but it's not for me, even with my own programming projects.

tweets.text:
I really got served hottt! 

I kept my phone inside my bag, wanting to carry the bag inside so I could keep it at the front of the exam hall, but one of the invigilators stopped me and said I should keep it outside. 

I did few calculations in my head like "before 1 hr I should be done now," so I went back outside and dropped my bag. My friend also kept her bag beside mine.

By the time we finished the exam and went to where we kept our bags to carry them, it was as if I was seeing double or I went blind for those seconds. Our bags had vanished into thin air. 

My friend's iPhone 14 was gone in the blink of an eye and my most prized Android phone., the one I dey guide with my life was also gone😭.

My friend almost went crazy, and I was being the bigger person until people started asking questions and I couldn’t help but cry while explaining.

It happened on the first day of my exams, so I had to move fast. Since I had also lost all my hard materials, I had to rely strictly on soft materials. 

I visited some friends for help; I got a friend to borrow me her laptop, logged into my WhatsApp, and asked my coursemates to send me our course materials so I could save them on the laptop. 

I later got another friend to borrow me his second phone just to stay connected. In conclusion, i survived 🥹

iPhone 13 vs iPhone 15 Camera Test — Is the Upgrade REALLY Worth It? 📸

At first glance, they look similar. But once you press the shutter, the difference becomes clear.

The iPhone 13 uses a 12MP main camera, while the iPhone 15 jumps to 48MP, delivering up to 4x more detail. That means sharper images, cleaner crops, and significantly better zoom results without losing quality.

And speaking of zoom, the iPhone 15 introduces 2x lossless zoom powered by its high-resolution sensor. No telephoto lens, yet it still achieves optical-quality results. On the iPhone 13, zooming in quickly reduces detail.

Low-light photography is another major shift. While both devices support Night Mode, the iPhone 15 is backed by a more advanced image processing system. The result is better light balance, more natural skin tones, and improved shadow detail.

Color and dynamic range also see noticeable improvements. The iPhone 13 delivers solid images, but the iPhone 15 produces richer colors, stronger contrast, and more accurate highlights, closer to what your eyes actually see.

Portrait photography has evolved too. The iPhone 15 offers improved edge detection, more realistic background blur, and the ability to adjust focus after taking the shot, something the iPhone 13 can’t match in complex scenes.

For video, both shoot in 4K and perform well, but the iPhone 15 benefits from better stabilization, cleaner HDR, and more consistent results in challenging lighting conditions.

So what’s the verdict?

If you take casual photos, the iPhone 13 still holds up. But if you care about detail, zoom, low-light performance, and content-quality output, the iPhone 15 is a clear upgrade.

This isn’t just a camera improvement. It’s the difference between taking photos and creating content.

Which one are you choosing, iPhone 13 or iPhone 15? 👇

After using the Samsung Galaxy S25 FE for over a week, I can confidently say it’s one of the best value for money Samsung phones right now.

For just $500, you’re getting:
• a smooth and polished UI
• solid, premium build quality
• impressive main and ultrawide cameras
• loud and clear speakers
• a great display

The downside of the Samsung Galaxy S25 FE is its pretty average battery life. If you’re a heavy user, you’ll likely need to charge it twice a day, and also the Exynos 2400 heats up quite easily on outdoors.
